content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

September 3, 1997


Richard K. Denhalter
Chief Deputy County Counsel
San Diego County Counsel
1600 Pacific Highway, Room 315
San Diego, California 92101

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-409

Dear Mr. Denhalter:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May an officeholder account contain more than $10,000?

CONCLUSION

Yes, because: (1) an officeholder may contribute personal funds in unlimited amounts to
his or her officeholder account (the $250 limit and the $10,000 aggregate limit do not apply), and
(2) Section 85313 does not contain an aggregate cap. Therefore, unspent funds may be rolled
over from one calendar year to the next without limiting how much an officeholder may add to
the account.

FACTS

A committee treasurer to an elected official in your jurisdiction has advised you that any
funds in an officeholder's account at year end can be rolled over and added to funds contributed
in the following year in an aggregate amount exceeding $10,000. You wish to clarify whether
this is correct.

_________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5560

File No. A-97-409
Page 2

ANALYSIS

Proposition 208 allows for a limited amount of fundraising each year ($10,000) for
expenses related to holding office. The blackout periods of Section 85305 do not apply to
officeholder contributions. (Ackerman Advice Letter, No. A-97-065.) Section 85313(a)
provides:

"Each elected officer may be permitted to establish one segregated
officeholder expense fund for expenses related to assisting,
serving, or communicating with constituents, or carrying out
the official duties of the elected officer, provided aggregate
contributions to such a fund do not exceed ten thousand dollars
($10,000) within any calendar year and that the expenditures are
not made in connection with any campaign for elective office or
ballot measure."

Accordingly, expenditures from officeholder accounts formed pursuant to Section
85313(a) must be used for expenses related to assisting, serving, or communicating with
constituents, or carrying out the official duties of the elected officer. (Johnson Advice Letter,
No. A-96-361a.) However, expenditures from officeholder accounts may not be made in
connection with any campaign for elective office or ballot measure.

Section 85313(b) limits the amount an elected official may accept in contributions from
any person to $250 and states that a contribution to an officeholder account shall not be
considered a campaign contribution. In addition Section 85313, unlike Section 85301 which
allows a candidate to contribute to his or her own campaign in unlimited amounts, does not
specifically give the elected officer the ability to contribute to his or her officeholder account in
an amount over $250 per calendar year. Nevertheless, we have concluded that the purposes of
the Act would not be furthered by applying the $250 per contributor limit or the $10,000 per year
aggregate limit to the officeholder. (Ackerman Advice Letter, No. A-97-267.) Therefore, we
have advised that an officeholder may contribute personal funds in unlimited amounts to his or
her own officeholder account. (Ackerman Advice Letter, supra; Markman Advice Letter, No. A-
97-235.)

In addition, according to Section 85313(a), a candidate may raise up to $10,000 in
contributions for the officeholder account each calendar year from sources other than the
officeholder's personal funds. Once the candidate raises this $10,000 into the account in any
given year, the candidate may not raise any additional funds into the account from sources other
than the officeholder's personal funds. In 1998 and later years, officeholders may carry over
unspent funds in the officeholder account from one calendar year to the next. (Johnson Advice
Letter, No. A-06-316a.) In 1998 and later years, an elected officer could raise up to $10,000 for
the officeholder account from sources other than the officeholder's personal funds and add that to

File No. A-97-409
Page 3

any unspent funds in the officeholder account.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Marte Castaños
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:MC:jlw

the account.
